pm-7.description: Cognigate integrates with enterprise architecture through its well-defined API boundary and deployment model: (1) API-first architecture — all Cognigate capabilities are exposed through RESTful APIs (/v1/intent, /v1/enforce, /v1/proof, /v1/agents, /v1/admin, /v1/health) enabling integration with enterprise systems; (2) Deployment flexibility — supports cloud deployment on Vercel (SaaS), self-hosted, and hybrid architectures; (3) Identity federation — the consumer organization's identity provider can support OIDC/SAML federation with enterprise identity providers; (4) Data integration — structured JSON API responses enable integration with enterprise SIEM, GRC, and monitoring systems; (5) Compliance integration — OSCAL-formatted documentation aligns with enterprise GRC tool requirements.
pm-7.remarks: Shared: Cognigate provides API-based integration points for enterprise architecture. Consumer organizations must incorporate Cognigate into their enterprise architecture documentation and ensure alignment with their EA framework.

pm-8.description: Cognigate supports critical infrastructure planning through its AI governance role in protecting automated systems: (1) AI agent governance — Cognigate governs AI agents that may interact with critical infrastructure systems, enforcing trust-based access control and behavioral constraints; (2) Graduated protection — the trust tier system (T0-T7) provides proportional access control for AI agents accessing critical systems; (3) Emergency controls — the circuit breaker provides immediate system-wide halt capability for critical infrastructure protection; (4) Evidence for planning — the proof chain provides auditable evidence of AI agent interactions with critical systems for infrastructure protection planning.
pm-8.remarks: Shared: Cognigate provides AI governance controls applicable to critical infrastructure protection. Consumer organizations must integrate Cognigate into their critical infrastructure protection plans and define appropriate trust tiers for agents interacting with critical systems.

pm-9.description: Cognigate implements risk management strategy through its multi-layered approach to AI agent risk: (1) Risk identification — the INTENT layer identifies risks through tripwire detection (regex security patterns in app/core/tripwires.py), paranoia mode (keyword analysis), and AI critic evaluation (app/core/critic.py); (2) Risk assessment — risk scores (0.0-1.0) quantify the level of risk for every agent request; (3) Risk response — the ENFORCE layer provides graduated risk response: ALLOW (accept risk), DENY (avoid risk), ESCALATE (transfer to human), SANDBOX (mitigate risk); (4) Risk monitoring — the PROOF layer continuously records risk decisions for trend analysis; the CA-7 health endpoint monitors risk posture; (5) Risk tolerance — rigor modes (STRICT, MODERATE, RELAXED) define organizational risk tolerance levels mapped to trust tiers.
pm-9.remarks: Shared: Cognigate provides a comprehensive risk management framework for AI agent governance. Consumer organizations must integrate Cognigate's risk management capabilities into their organizational risk management strategy and define acceptable risk tolerance levels.

pm-10.description: Cognigate supports the authorization process through its OSCAL-formatted compliance documentation and continuous monitoring capabilities: (1) Authorization documentation — OSCAL component-definition.json, SSP draft, and POA&M provide the documentation package for authorization; (2) Continuous authorization — the CA-7 health endpoint provides ongoing security posture assessment supporting continuous ATO; (3) Control implementation evidence — the proof chain generates cryptographically verified evidence of control effectiveness; (4) Risk assessment — the INTENT layer's continuous risk scoring supports ongoing risk evaluation required for authorization decisions.
pm-10.remarks: Shared: Cognigate provides authorization-ready documentation and continuous monitoring. Consumer organizations must initiate and manage the authorization process with their authorizing official and integrate Cognigate's documentation into their authorization package.

pm-11.description: Cognigate defines its mission and business processes in the context of AI agent governance: (1) Mission — provide runtime governance, trust-based access control, and cryptographic audit trails for AI agent operations; (2) Business processes — entity registration and identity management, trust scoring and tier progression, policy evaluation and enforcement, incident detection and response, evidence generation and compliance reporting; (3) Process protection — each business process is protected by the three-layer architecture: INTENT (risk analysis), ENFORCE (access control), PROOF (audit trail); (4) Process integration — the capability taxonomy (100+ capabilities, 8 namespaces) defines how business processes interact with AI agent operations.
pm-11.remarks: Shared: Cognigate defines and protects its core business processes through the INTENT/ENFORCE/PROOF architecture. Consumer organizations must define how Cognigate's processes integrate with their organizational mission and business processes.

pm-13.description: Cognigate supports information security workforce development through its documentation and tooling: (1) Developer security training — the CI/CD pipeline with Semgrep SAST and CodeQL provides real-time security feedback to developers, serving as continuous security education; (2) Security documentation — the BASIS specification, ADRs, and API documentation provide security architecture knowledge for operations staff; (3) Compliance knowledge — the evidence mapper (234 rules, 9 frameworks) educates security personnel on compliance requirements; (4) Operational security skills — the circuit breaker manual controls, trust management, and proof chain verification tools develop incident response and security monitoring skills.
pm-13.remarks: Shared: Cognigate provides security-focused documentation and tooling that supports workforce development. Consumer organizations must establish formal security workforce development programs and ensure personnel are trained on Cognigate-specific security procedures.

pm-14.description: Cognigate implements testing, training, and monitoring processes: (1) Testing — the CI/CD pipeline enforces 85%+ test coverage with pytest, mutation testing via mutmut (mutmut_config.py), SAST scanning (Semgrep, CodeQL), secret detection (Gitleaks), and dependency auditing (pip-audit); incident response testing via circuit breaker manual controls; (2) Training — operational documentation in the BASIS specification, API reference, and ADRs provides training material; the evidence mapper educates on compliance requirements; (3) Monitoring — the CA-7 health endpoint (/v1/health) provides continuous monitoring across 9 compliance frameworks; the proof chain provides real-time security event monitoring; velocity tracking monitors usage patterns. These processes are integrated through the evidence mapper which correlates testing, training, and monitoring activities with compliance requirements.
pm-14.remarks: Shared: Cognigate provides integrated testing, training support, and monitoring capabilities. Consumer organizations must establish testing schedules, training programs, and monitoring procedures that incorporate Cognigate's capabilities.

pm-17.description: Cognigate protects controlled unclassified information (CUI) on external systems through its trust-tiered access model: (1) External agent restrictions — AI agents from external systems are registered at T0 (Sandbox) with access limited to sandbox-namespace capabilities, preventing access to CUI; (2) Data classification — the capability taxonomy defines data access tiers that prevent lower-trust entities from accessing classified or CUI data; (3) Cross-boundary controls — policy rules restrict data:read and data:write capabilities based on trust tier, preventing CUI from flowing to unauthorized external systems; (4) Evidence — all data access decisions are recorded in the proof chain for CUI handling accountability.
pm-17.remarks: Shared: Cognigate provides trust-based access controls that restrict CUI access to authorized trust tiers. Consumer organizations must define which data constitutes CUI and configure appropriate trust tier requirements and policy rules.

pm-18.description: Cognigate supports privacy program planning through its data handling controls and transparency mechanisms: (1) Privacy-aware design — the PROOF layer records all data processing activities with cryptographic integrity, supporting privacy accountability; (2) Data minimization — the capability taxonomy restricts data access to the minimum necessary for each trust tier; (3) Privacy documentation — the OSCAL documentation includes privacy control implementations mapped to the NIST privacy framework; (4) Privacy monitoring — the evidence mapper includes privacy-relevant compliance rules (GDPR, CCPA mappings) for continuous privacy posture assessment.
pm-18.remarks: Shared: Cognigate provides privacy-supportive technical controls and documentation. Consumer organizations must develop a comprehensive privacy program plan that incorporates Cognigate's privacy controls and addresses organizational privacy requirements.

pm-19.description: Cognigate supports organizational privacy program leadership through its privacy-aware architecture: (1) Privacy accountability — the proof chain creates an auditable record of all data processing decisions, supporting the privacy officer's oversight responsibilities; (2) Privacy controls — trust-tiered access, capability-based authorization, and data classification provide technical privacy controls that the privacy leader can point to as organizational safeguards; (3) Privacy reporting — the evidence mapper generates privacy-relevant compliance reports across GDPR, CCPA, and other privacy frameworks.
pm-19.remarks: Shared: Cognigate provides technical privacy controls and accountability mechanisms. Consumer organizations must designate a senior official for privacy and ensure that official has authority and resources to oversee privacy requirements. This is primarily an organizational control.

pm-20.description: Cognigate supports dissemination of privacy program information through transparency mechanisms: (1) API transparency — all enforcement decisions include the reason for the decision (verdict rationale) in API responses, enabling data subjects to understand processing decisions; (2) Proof chain access — authorized entities can query their own proof chain records via the /v1/proof endpoint, providing transparency into how their data was processed; (3) Privacy documentation — the BASIS specification and OSCAL documentation describe Cognigate's privacy controls and data handling practices; (4) Compliance reporting — the evidence mapper generates privacy compliance reports that can be shared with stakeholders.
pm-20.remarks: Shared: Cognigate provides transparency mechanisms for data processing decisions. Consumer organizations must develop and publish privacy program information (privacy policies, privacy impact assessments, data processing agreements) for their stakeholders.

pm-21.description: Cognigate supports accounting of disclosures through its proof chain and evidence mapper: (1) Data access logging — every data access decision (data:read, data:write capabilities) is recorded in the proof chain with the accessing entity, timestamp, data classification, and authorization basis; (2) Disclosure tracking — when data is shared through comm:external capabilities, the proof chain records the recipient, purpose, and authorization; (3) Evidence integrity — all disclosure records are protected by SHA-256 hash chain integrity and Ed25519 signatures, ensuring disclosure accounting records cannot be tampered with; (4) Retention — proof chain records are retained for 7 years, exceeding typical disclosure accounting retention requirements.
pm-21.remarks: Shared: Cognigate provides cryptographically verified records of data access and sharing. Consumer organizations must develop procedures for responding to disclosure accounting requests and integrate Cognigate's proof chain data into their disclosure tracking process.

pm-22.description: Cognigate supports PII quality management through its data validation and integrity mechanisms: (1) Input validation — all API endpoints validate input data format, type, and constraints before processing, ensuring data quality at ingestion; (2) Data integrity — the proof chain's SHA-256 hash chain verification ensures data integrity throughout its lifecycle; (3) Entity data accuracy — entity registration data (entity_id, public key, trust tier) is validated at registration and any changes are recorded with TRUST_DELTA proof events; (4) Data correction — entities can be updated via the admin API with all changes recorded in the proof chain for audit.
pm-22.remarks: Shared: Cognigate provides data validation and integrity verification. Consumer organizations must establish PII quality management procedures and ensure that PII processed through Cognigate meets their quality standards.

pm-24.description: Cognigate's data integrity controls support organizational data integrity board functions: (1) Data matching controls — the entity registration system uses exact-match validation for entity_id and public key lookups, with uniqueness constraints enforced at the database level; (2) Data integrity verification — the proof chain provides cryptographic verification of all data modifications; (3) Dispute resolution evidence — the proof chain's immutable, timestamped, cryptographically signed records provide authoritative evidence for data integrity disputes.
pm-24.remarks: Shared: Cognigate provides data integrity controls and evidence. The establishment and operation of a Data Integrity Board is an organizational responsibility for consumer organizations subject to the Computer Matching and Privacy Protection Act.

pm-25.description: Cognigate minimizes PII in testing, training, and research through its development practices: (1) Test data — pytest test suites use synthetic entity_ids, generated Ed25519 key pairs, and fabricated trust scores; no production PII is used in testing; (2) Development database — local development database (cognigate.db) contains only synthetic test data; (3) CI/CD isolation — GitHub Actions test environments use ephemeral databases with synthetic data; (4) Data generation — test fixtures generate random entity identifiers, key pairs, and policy configurations rather than copying production data.
pm-25.remarks: Provided: Cognigate's test suite uses exclusively synthetic data. No PII is used in testing, training, or research activities.

pm-26.description: Cognigate supports complaint management through its transparency and evidence mechanisms: (1) Decision transparency — all enforcement decisions (ALLOW, DENY, ESCALATE, SANDBOX verdicts) include the rationale and policy rule that triggered the decision, enabling complainants to understand adverse decisions; (2) Evidence access — the proof chain provides cryptographically verified evidence supporting or refuting complaints about system behavior; (3) Administrative review — the admin API (/v1/admin) allows authorized personnel to investigate complaints by reviewing entity trust history, policy evaluation records, and proof chain events. A formal complaint management process is an organizational responsibility.
pm-26.remarks: Shared: Cognigate provides transparency and evidence for complaint investigation. Consumer organizations must establish formal complaint management procedures, designate complaint handlers, and respond to complaints within required timeframes.

pm-27.description: Cognigate supports privacy reporting through its compliance monitoring and evidence generation capabilities: (1) Privacy metrics — the evidence mapper (app/core/evidence_mapper.py) generates privacy-specific compliance reports covering GDPR, CCPA, and NIST Privacy Framework requirements; (2) Data processing records — the proof chain provides auditable records of all data processing activities for privacy reporting; (3) Incident reporting — privacy-related incidents (data access violations, unauthorized disclosures) are captured in the proof chain and reported through the health endpoint; (4) Compliance dashboards — the CA-7 health endpoint includes privacy framework compliance status.
pm-27.remarks: Shared: Cognigate provides privacy-relevant metrics and evidence for reporting. Consumer organizations must establish privacy reporting procedures, define reporting frequency and recipients, and compile reports using Cognigate's data.

pm-28.description: Cognigate implements risk framing for its AI agent governance domain: (1) Risk context — the INTENT layer defines the risk context for AI agent operations, including threat categories (malicious input, credential abuse, data exfiltration, policy circumvention) and vulnerability patterns (tripwire patterns in app/core/tripwires.py); (2) Risk assumptions — the trust tier model (T0-T7) embodies the assumption that unknown agents present higher risk than established agents, with trust earned through demonstrated safe behavior; (3) Risk constraints — rigor modes (STRICT, MODERATE, RELAXED) define organizational risk tolerance boundaries; (4) Risk tolerance — the risk scoring threshold (configurable, default 0.7) defines the acceptable risk level for automated processing; scores above the threshold trigger escalation or denial.
pm-28.remarks: Shared: Cognigate provides a comprehensive risk framing model for AI agent governance. Consumer organizations must integrate Cognigate's risk framing into their organizational risk management framework and define risk tolerance levels appropriate for their operational context.

pm-31.description: Cognigate implements continuous monitoring strategy through its CA-7 health endpoint and PROOF layer: (1) Continuous monitoring — the /v1/health endpoint (app/routers/health.py, app/core/control_health.py) provides real-time security posture assessment across 9 compliance frameworks (NIST 800-53, SOC 2, ISO 27001, GDPR, HIPAA, FedRAMP, PCI-DSS, CCPA, AI-specific); (2) Ongoing assessment — the evidence mapper (app/core/evidence_mapper.py, 234 rules) continuously maps system events to compliance requirements, identifying gaps in real-time; (3) Monitoring metrics — circuit breaker state, velocity utilization, trust score distribution, authentication success/failure rates, and proof chain integrity status are continuously monitored; (4) Alert generation — threshold-based alerts for anomalous patterns (high risk scores, velocity exceedances, authentication failures) are delivered via webhooks. The monitoring strategy aligns with NIST SP 800-137 (ISCM) and supports FedRAMP continuous monitoring requirements.
pm-31.remarks: Shared: Cognigate provides a comprehensive continuous monitoring implementation through the CA-7 health endpoint and evidence mapper. Consumer organizations must define their continuous monitoring strategy, configure monitoring thresholds, and integrate Cognigate's monitoring data into their organizational ISCM program.

ps-1.description: Cognigate supports personnel security policy through its identity management and audit trail capabilities: (1) Entity lifecycle — the /v1/agents API manages the complete lifecycle of AI agent 'personnel' (registration, trust progression, suspension, deregistration) with cryptographic accountability; (2) Access control — trust-tiered access (T0-T7) implements the principle that personnel access is proportional to demonstrated trustworthiness; (3) Audit trail — all entity actions are recorded in the immutable proof chain, providing personnel activity accountability; (4) Policy enforcement — the ENFORCE layer's policy engine evaluates behavioral constraints for all entities, serving as automated personnel conduct enforcement. Organizational personnel security policies (hiring, screening, termination procedures for human staff) are outside Cognigate's scope.
ps-1.remarks: Shared: Cognigate provides automated personnel security controls for AI agent entities. Consumer organizations must develop and maintain personnel security policies for their human staff and integrate Cognigate's entity management into their overall personnel security program.

ps-2.description: Cognigate supports position risk designation through its trust tier model: (1) Risk categorization — the 8 trust tiers (T0-T7) categorize entity 'positions' by risk level: T0-T1 (Sandbox/Minimal — lowest risk), T2-T3 (Basic/Standard — low-moderate risk), T4-T5 (Elevated/Trusted — moderate-high risk), T6-T7 (Privileged/System — highest risk); (2) Capability mapping — each trust tier maps to a defined set of capabilities across 8 namespaces, with higher tiers having access to more sensitive capabilities; (3) Risk review — trust tiers are documented in the BASIS specification with clear criteria for tier assignment and progression; (4) Screening requirements — higher trust tiers require more evidence of trustworthy behavior (182-day trust decay ensures ongoing validation). For organizational positions, consumer organizations must perform position risk designation for their human personnel.
ps-2.remarks: Shared: Cognigate implements automated position risk designation for AI agent entities through the trust tier model. Consumer organizations must perform formal position risk designation and screening level assignment for their human personnel positions.

ps-3.description: Cognigate supports personnel screening through its trust-based validation system for AI agent entities: (1) Initial screening — new entities are registered at T0 (Sandbox) by default, providing a probationary period where the entity's behavior is monitored with maximum restrictions; (2) Progressive screening — trust score accumulation from T0 to T7 serves as ongoing behavioral screening; each tier boundary crossing represents additional screening validation; (3) Re-screening — the 182-day trust decay mechanism requires entities to continuously demonstrate trustworthy behavior to maintain their trust tier; inactive entities naturally decay to lower tiers; (4) Screening evidence — the proof chain records all trust score changes (TRUST_DELTA events) providing a complete screening history. Human personnel screening (background investigations) is an organizational responsibility outside Cognigate's scope.
ps-3.remarks: Shared: Cognigate implements automated behavioral screening for AI agent entities through trust scoring and decay. Consumer organizations must conduct formal background investigations and screening for human personnel per their position risk designations.

ps-4.description: Cognigate supports personnel termination procedures through its entity deregistration and access revocation capabilities: (1) Access revocation — entity deregistration via the admin API immediately revokes all capabilities, invalidates authentication credentials (Ed25519 public key removed, API key hash deleted), and prevents further system access; (2) Session termination — active sessions for a deregistered entity are immediately invalidated; the circuit breaker can enforce system-wide session termination if needed; (3) Credential recovery — upon deregistration, the entity's public key binding is severed, preventing use of the entity's credentials for future access; (4) Evidence preservation — the deregistered entity's complete proof chain history is retained for 7 years, preserving all activity records for post-termination audit; (5) Exit notification — deregistration events generate ENTITY_DEREGISTERED proof records with the deregistering administrator's identity, timestamp, and reason.
ps-4.remarks: Shared: Cognigate provides automated access revocation and evidence preservation for entity termination. Consumer organizations are responsible for conducting termination procedures for human personnel and ensuring timely notification to Cognigate administrators for entity deregistration.

ps-5.description: Cognigate supports personnel transfer procedures through its trust management and capability reassignment features: (1) Trust tier adjustment — when an entity's role changes, administrators can adjust the entity's trust tier via the admin API, immediately modifying the entity's capability set to match the new role; (2) Capability reassignment — the policy engine supports role-based policy rules that automatically adjust capabilities when an entity's metadata (role, department, function) is updated; (3) Transfer evidence — trust tier changes generate TRUST_DELTA proof records documenting the old tier, new tier, reason for change, and authorizing administrator; (4) Access review — the /v1/admin/entities endpoint provides entity enumeration with current trust tiers and capabilities for transfer-related access reviews.
ps-5.remarks: Shared: Cognigate provides trust tier adjustment and capability reassignment for entity role changes. Consumer organizations must establish personnel transfer procedures and ensure timely notification to Cognigate administrators when personnel roles change.

ps-6.description: Cognigate enforces access agreements through its entity registration and policy enforcement model: (1) Registration agreement — entity registration at /v1/agents constitutes an implicit access agreement where the entity accepts the behavioral constraints defined by its trust tier, applicable policies, and the capability taxonomy; (2) Policy acceptance — the ENFORCE layer's policy engine evaluates every request against defined policies; entities that violate policies face trust score reduction, velocity throttling, and potential circuit breaker trip; (3) Agreement documentation — the proof chain records the registration event (ENTITY_REGISTERED) with the applicable policy set and trust tier constraints, serving as the access agreement record; (4) Agreement review — trust tier changes and policy updates require entities to operate under updated constraints, which are automatically enforced.
ps-6.remarks: Shared: Cognigate programmatically enforces behavioral agreements for AI agent entities through policy evaluation. Consumer organizations must maintain formal access agreements for human personnel, including non-disclosure agreements and acceptable use policies, and ensure agreements are reviewed and updated.

ps-7.description: Cognigate addresses external personnel security through its trust tier system for third-party AI agents: (1) External entity management — third-party AI agents register at /v1/agents and receive T0 (Sandbox) trust tier by default, restricting them to the most limited capability set; (2) Access restrictions — external entities are subject to the same ENFORCE pipeline as internal entities, with policy rules that can apply additional restrictions for external entity types; (3) Monitoring — all external entity actions are logged in the proof chain with the same level of detail as internal entities; (4) Termination — external entity access can be immediately revoked via the admin API. For external human personnel (contractors, vendors), consumer organizations must establish their own personnel security requirements.
ps-7.remarks: Shared: Cognigate manages external AI agent access through its trust tier system with default T0 restrictions. Consumer organizations must establish personnel security requirements for their external human personnel and monitor compliance with those requirements.

ps-8.description: Cognigate implements sanctions for AI agent entity policy violations through automated enforcement mechanisms: (1) Trust score reduction — policy violations trigger automatic trust score decreases (TRUST_DELTA proof events with negative deltas), potentially causing tier demotion and capability loss; (2) Velocity throttling — entities exceeding rate limits are automatically throttled via the velocity cap system (app/core/velocity.py); (3) Circuit breaker sanctions — severe violations (POLICY_VIOLATION, TRUST_BREACH trip reasons) trigger circuit breaker action that can suspend entity access; (4) Progressive sanctions — the circuit breaker's graduated state machine (WARN -> THROTTLE -> RESTRICT -> SUSPEND -> OPEN) implements proportional sanctions; (5) Sanction records — all sanctions are recorded in the proof chain with the violation details, sanction applied, and enforcing mechanism. Human personnel sanctions are an organizational responsibility.
ps-8.remarks: Shared: Cognigate provides automated sanctions for AI agent entity policy violations through trust score reduction, throttling, and circuit breaker enforcement. Consumer organizations must establish formal personnel sanctions processes for human staff and ensure consistency with organizational personnel policies.

ps-9.description: Cognigate supports position descriptions through its capability taxonomy and trust tier documentation: (1) Entity role definitions — each trust tier (T0-T7) defines a clear set of authorized capabilities across 8 namespaces (sandbox, data, comm, execute, financial, admin, efficiency, custom), serving as the 'position description' for AI agent entities; (2) Security responsibilities — each tier's capability set implicitly defines the entity's security responsibilities and access boundaries; (3) Documentation — the BASIS specification documents tier requirements, capability mappings, and behavioral expectations for each trust level. Human personnel position descriptions with security role assignments are an organizational responsibility.
ps-9.remarks: Shared: Cognigate defines 'position descriptions' for AI agent entities through the trust tier and capability taxonomy. Consumer organizations must create formal position descriptions for human personnel that include security roles and responsibilities.

pt-1.description: Cognigate's PII processing and transparency policy is implemented through its data handling controls and evidence mechanisms: (1) Processing policy — the capability taxonomy defines what data operations each trust tier can perform (data:read, data:write scoped by classification); these capability definitions serve as the PII processing policy; (2) Transparency — all data processing decisions are recorded in the proof chain with the processing purpose, authorization basis, and decision outcome; (3) Policy documentation — the BASIS specification documents data handling practices and privacy-relevant controls; (4) Policy review — the evidence mapper includes privacy framework mappings (GDPR, CCPA) enabling automated privacy policy compliance assessment through the CA-7 health endpoint.
pt-1.remarks: Shared: Cognigate provides PII processing controls and transparency mechanisms through its capability taxonomy and proof chain. Consumer organizations must develop comprehensive PII processing policies, conduct privacy impact assessments, and ensure processing aligns with applicable privacy regulations.

pt-2.description: Cognigate processes limited PII in its normal operations: (1) Entity registration data — entity_id, public key, trust tier, and organizational affiliation are collected at registration; (2) Processing authority — data processing is authorized through the capability taxonomy; entities can only process data types authorized by their trust tier; (3) Authority documentation — the BASIS specification documents the types of data Cognigate processes and the authorization basis; (4) Proof chain records — all data processing events include the authorization basis (trust tier, policy rule, capability grant) providing traceable authority for each processing action. Cognigate does not process traditional PII (names, addresses, SSNs) — it processes AI agent operational data (entity identifiers, trust scores, capability requests, risk assessments).
pt-2.remarks: Shared: Cognigate's PII processing is limited to entity operational data with documented authority. Consumer organizations must ensure they have legal authority to process any PII that flows through Cognigate as part of their AI agent operations and document that authority.

pt-3.description: Cognigate restricts PII processing to defined purposes through its capability-based authorization model: (1) Purpose limitation — each capability in the taxonomy defines a specific processing purpose (e.g., data:read for data retrieval, data:write for data modification); entities can only process data for purposes authorized by their granted capabilities; (2) Purpose enforcement — the ENFORCE layer validates that every data processing request matches an authorized purpose before allowing execution; mismatched purposes generate DENY verdicts; (3) Purpose documentation — the proof chain records the stated purpose and authorized capability for every data processing action; (4) Purpose auditing — proof chain queries can filter by capability/purpose to verify that processing was limited to authorized purposes.
pt-3.remarks: Shared: Cognigate enforces purpose limitations through capability-based authorization. Consumer organizations must define the specific purposes for which PII may be processed and configure Cognigate's capability grants accordingly.

pt-4.description: Cognigate supports consent mechanisms for data processing through its trust tier and policy framework: (1) Implicit consent — entity registration at /v1/agents constitutes consent to Cognigate's data processing as defined by the entity's trust tier and applicable policies; (2) Consent records — the proof chain records registration events (ENTITY_REGISTERED) with the applicable data processing scope, serving as a consent record; (3) Consent withdrawal — entity deregistration serves as consent withdrawal, immediately revoking all data processing capabilities; (4) Consent granularity — the capability taxonomy enables granular consent: entities can be granted specific data processing capabilities (data:read without data:write) reflecting granular consent decisions.
pt-4.remarks: Shared: Cognigate provides consent-like mechanisms through entity registration and capability grants. Consumer organizations must implement formal consent collection mechanisms (privacy notices, consent forms) for human data subjects whose PII may be processed by Cognigate, and ensure consent is obtained before processing.

pt-5.description: Cognigate provides privacy notices through its API transparency mechanisms: (1) Decision transparency — every enforcement decision returned by the ENFORCE layer includes the verdict (ALLOW, DENY, ESCALATE, SANDBOX), the triggering policy rule, and the risk assessment, providing real-time notice of how requests are processed; (2) Registration disclosure — entity registration responses include the trust tier assignment and applicable capability restrictions, notifying the entity of the data processing scope; (3) API documentation — the BASIS specification and API reference document all data collection, processing, and sharing practices; (4) Proof chain access — entities can query their own proof chain records via the /v1/proof endpoint to review how their data has been processed.
pt-5.remarks: Shared: Cognigate provides real-time processing transparency through API responses and proof chain access. Consumer organizations must provide formal privacy notices to individuals whose PII is processed, including notice of Cognigate's role in data processing.

pt-5.2.description: Cognigate supports Privacy Act statement requirements through its documentation and evidence generation: (1) Processing documentation — the OSCAL documentation and BASIS specification describe the types of data collected, the legal authority for collection, and the purposes for processing; (2) Evidence — the proof chain provides verifiable records of actual data processing aligned with stated purposes. Consumer organizations that operate Privacy Act systems of records must include Privacy Act statements on their forms and integrate Cognigate's processing disclosure into those statements.
pt-5.2.remarks: Shared: Cognigate provides processing documentation that can be incorporated into Privacy Act statements. The requirement to include Privacy Act statements on forms is an organizational responsibility applicable to federal agencies operating systems of records. Not directly applicable to Cognigate as a software product.

pt-6.description: Cognigate supports System of Records Notice (SORN) requirements through its data processing transparency: (1) Data inventory — Cognigate's data model is fully documented in the API specification and models (app/models/), identifying all data elements collected and processed; (2) Processing documentation — the OSCAL documentation describes data types, purposes, retention periods (7-year proof chain retention), and sharing practices; (3) Evidence for SORN — the proof chain provides verifiable evidence of actual data processing that can be referenced in SORN publications. Cognigate itself does not maintain a Privacy Act system of records — it processes AI agent operational data.
pt-6.remarks: Shared: Cognigate provides data processing documentation suitable for SORN reference. The publication of System of Records Notices is a federal agency organizational responsibility. Not directly applicable to Cognigate as a commercial software product.

pt-7.description: Cognigate addresses specific categories of PII through its data classification system: (1) Data classification — the capability taxonomy classifies data into access tiers (sandbox, operational, classified, administrative) with each tier defining the sensitivity level; (2) Special category protections — the policy engine can enforce additional restrictions for processing specific data categories through configurable policy rules; (3) Processing restrictions — trust-tiered access ensures that sensitive data categories are only accessible to entities with appropriate trust levels (T4+ for classified data, T6+ for administrative data); (4) Audit — all data processing involving specific categories is recorded in the proof chain with the data classification level.
pt-7.remarks: Shared: Cognigate provides data classification and access control mechanisms. Consumer organizations must identify specific categories of PII that are processed through Cognigate, apply appropriate sensitivity classifications, and configure policy rules for any additional protections required.

pt-8.description: Cognigate's architecture supports computer matching program requirements through its data integrity and accountability controls: (1) Data matching controls — entity lookups use exact-match identifiers (entity_id, public key) with database-level uniqueness constraints preventing misidentification; (2) Matching accountability — the proof chain records all data access and matching operations with the requesting entity, purpose, and result; (3) Matching integrity — SHA-256 hash chain verification ensures matching data has not been altered. Computer matching program establishment is a federal agency organizational responsibility under the Computer Matching and Privacy Protection Act.
pt-8.remarks: Shared: Cognigate provides data integrity controls that support computer matching programs. Computer matching agreements and oversight are federal agency organizational responsibilities. Not directly applicable to Cognigate as a commercial software product.
